old_claim.note: Data Compression provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: TCP/IP transports application octets without requiring that payloads be compressed, so compression remains an optional application or efficiency technique.

source_shape.source_locator: Section 1.5, Basic Data Transfer (p. 3), and section 2.8, Data Communication (p. 12), specify a continuous octet stream packaged into TCP segments and enumerate TCP's required transport functions without a compression stage.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: RFC 793 defines TCP as reliable transport of an application-provided octet stream, segmented independently of the payload's representation.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Compression can reduce application data volume, but TCP accepts arbitrary octets and provides reliability, sequencing, flow control, and multiplexing without depending on compression.

ontology_before: The Internet node treated an optional payload optimization as a direct prerequisite of the TCP/IP internetwork.
ontology_after: Data compression remains an optional application-layer or efficiency technology outside the direct architecture of the 1983 Internet node.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-internet-operational-boundary: internet no longer directly depends on data_compression.
would_reject_if: The 1983 Internet scope is narrowed to a service whose protocol specification mandates compression before TCP transport.
short_reason: RFC 793 transports an arbitrary octet stream and does not require compression, so compression is optional rather than architectural.
